sections.header: Verses 1–15 — "Do Not Trust in Deceptive Words"

heb.paragraph: The people chant "the temple of the LORD, the temple of the LORD, the temple of the LORD" (hêkal YHWH × 3, v.4) — a liturgical formula treated as a talisman. The triple repetition mimics cultic invocation, but Jeremiah calls it "deceptive words" (dibrê haššeqer). The temple has become an idol — its presence is trusted to guarantee Jerusalem's safety regardless of moral conduct. This is the theology of automatic divine protection that Jeremiah demolishes.

God asks: "Has this house become a den of robbers (mĕʿārat pārîṣîm) in your eyes?" (v.11). The temple is not merely profaned by bad worship but weaponized as moral cover — criminals use it as a hideout, treating its sanctity as protection from accountability. Jesus quotes this exact phrase when cleansing the temple (Matt 21:13).

panels.ctx: The Temple Sermon (ch. 7) is one of the most important speeches in the Hebrew Bible. Jeremiah stands at the gate of the temple and attacks the foundational belief of Jerusalem theology: that God's presence in the temple guarantees the city's inviolability. This "Zion theology" — rooted in legitimate traditions about God choosing Jerusalem (2 Sam 7, Ps 132) — had become distorted into a form of magical thinking. Jeremiah invokes the precedent of Shiloh (v.12), where God's earlier dwelling place was destroyed because of Israel's sin. If God abandoned Shiloh, he can abandon the temple. The prose form of this sermon appears in chapter 26, which records the assassination attempt it provoked.

cross.note: The Ark brought to battle as a talisman — and captured by the Philistines. Shiloh's destruction proves that God's presence is not unconditionally tied to any structure.
Jesus quotes Jeremiah 7:11 directly: "My house shall be called a house of prayer, but you have made it a den of robbers." The temple cleansing reenacts Jeremiah's sermon.
Stephen's speech before the Sanhedrin reprises Jeremiah's argument: God does not dwell in buildings made by human hands.

notes.note: Jeremiah stands at the temple gate — a confrontational position that challenges the entire cultic establishment. The threefold chant "the temple of the LORD" is exposed as self-deception, not genuine worship.
The alternative: genuine reform — justice, protection of aliens/orphans/widows, and abandonment of idolatry. These are the conditions for continued residence in the land, not temple attendance.

The devastating list: steal, murder, commit adultery, perjure, burn incense to Baal — then come and stand in God's house saying "We are safe!" The temple has become a criminals' refuge, sanitizing wickedness by proximity to holiness.

The Shiloh precedent: God's first dwelling among Israel was destroyed. Archaeological evidence confirms Shiloh's destruction c. 1050 BC. If God abandoned Shiloh, the temple enjoys no automatic immunity.

Calvin sees the threefold "temple of the LORD" as a warning against all forms of sacramentalism — trusting in religious structures, rituals, or institutions rather than in God himself. The temple is a means of grace, not a substitute for grace.

Calvin comments: "God could endure the temple being profaned, but he could not endure it being made a cloak for wickedness." The worst use of sacred things is to employ them as moral cover for injustice.

The threefold repetition hêkal YHWH may reflect an actual liturgical formula used in temple worship — a confessional chant that affirmed God's protective presence. Jeremiah subverts the liturgy by calling it "deceptive."

Shiloh (modern Khirbet Seilun) was the central sanctuary during the period of the judges (Josh 18:1; 1 Sam 1–4). Its destruction — not mentioned in the Hebrew Bible but confirmed by archaeology — became a paradigmatic warning in prophetic rhetoric.
The reference to "all your fellow Israelites, all the people of Ephraim" alludes to the northern kingdom's exile in 722 BC. Judah faces the same fate as the northern tribes unless they repent.

Lundbom calls this "the most important sermon in the Hebrew Bible." He identifies its structure as a prophetic disputation: Jeremiah states the people's false premise (v.4), offers the true condition for security (vv.5–7), exposes the current reality (vv.8–11), and delivers the precedent-based verdict (vv.12–15). The rhetorical force depends on the audience's knowledge that Shiloh was destroyed.

Lundbom notes that mĕʿārat pārîṣîm ("den of robbers") does not mean a place where robbery occurs but a place where robbers go to feel safe after committing their crimes. The temple provides psychological cover, not ethical transformation.

Brueggemann reads the Temple Sermon as a frontal assault on "royal-temple ideology" — the belief system that legitimates power through religious sanction. Jeremiah exposes the temple as an instrument of social control rather than a site of genuine encounter with God.

sections.header: Verses 16–29 — The Queen of Heaven and Disobedience

heb.paragraph: Women and children make cakes "for the queen of heaven" (meleket haššāmayim, v.18) — likely Ishtar/Astarte, the Mesopotamian goddess of love and war. The cult involved the entire family: children gather wood, fathers kindle the fire, women knead dough. The detail reveals how deeply syncretism had penetrated domestic life — it was not a priestly deviation but a household practice.

panels.ctx: God commands Jeremiah not to pray for this people (v.16) — one of the most shocking directives in the book. The prophetic intercessory role, exemplified by Moses and Samuel, is suspended. The reason follows: the apostasy has become total, involving every member of the household in the worship of the queen of heaven. The section transitions to a broader theological statement: God never commanded burnt offerings in the exodus — what he demanded was obedience (vv.22–23). This does not abolish sacrifice but relativizes it: ritual without obedience is meaningless.

cross.note: In exile, the women explicitly defend their worship of the queen of heaven, claiming prosperity when they did so — a later passage that shows how entrenched this cult was.
"To obey is better than sacrifice" — the same prophetic principle that Jeremiah reasserts in vv.22–23.
Micah asks what God requires: not thousands of rams or rivers of oil, but justice, mercy, and humble walking with God — the same prophetic reordering of priorities.

notes.note: "Do not pray for this people" — an extraordinary prohibition. Prophetic intercession was a key function (cf. Moses in Exod 32:11–14, Samuel in 1 Sam 7:5–9). Suspending it signals that the time for intercession has passed. Judgment is now irrevocable.

The family participation in queen of heaven worship shows that syncretism was not a marginal practice but a domestic tradition. Children, mothers, and fathers all had assigned roles. Paganism had become normalized.

God's statement "I did not give commands about burnt offerings" does not deny the Levitical system but subordinates it. The primary demand at Sinai was "Obey me, and I will be your God" — the covenant relationship precedes and grounds all ritual prescriptions.

Calvin wrestles with the prohibition of prayer. He concludes that God is not forbidding all prayer but prophetic intercession that seeks to avert judgment. The decree has been sealed; intercession would be futile. Yet even in this dark hour, individual repentance remains possible.

Calvin uses this passage to argue that ceremonies are never ends in themselves but means to obedience. When ritual replaces the ethical demand it was designed to serve, it becomes an obstacle to genuine religion.

The "queen of heaven" (meleket haššāmayim) is most likely Ishtar, the Mesopotamian goddess of love and war, identified with the West Semitic Astarte. The cake-baking ritual is attested in Mesopotamian cult texts for Ishtar.

The statement "I did not give commands about burnt offerings" is a rhetorical prioritization, not a historical denial. The Hebrew idiom lōʾ X kî ʾim Y ("not X but Y") often means "not so much X as Y" — the emphasis is on priority, not exclusion.

Lundbom identifies the prohibition of prayer as the theological turning point of the sermon. Up to this point, intercession remained possible. From here on, the prophetic ministry shifts from mediation to witness — Jeremiah will testify to the justice of the judgment he cannot prevent.

Brueggemann reads this as a radical prophetic hermeneutic — Jeremiah reinterprets the Sinai tradition to subordinate the entire sacrificial system to the simple demand for obedience. This is not anti-ritual polemic but a prophetic recentering of the covenant relationship on its relational core rather than its institutional expression.

heb.paragraph: Topheth (tōpet, v.31) was the site in the Valley of Ben Hinnom (gê ben-hinnōm → Gehenna) where children were sacrificed by fire to Molech. The word may derive from a root meaning "fireplace" or from tōp ("drum") — drums were beaten to drown out the screams of the children. This valley south of Jerusalem became a symbol of divine judgment so potent that by the intertestamental period "Gehenna" was the standard term for eschatological punishment.

panels.ctx: The sermon reaches its horrific climax with the accusation of child sacrifice at Topheth in the Valley of Ben Hinnom. This was the ultimate covenant violation — offering children to Molech, something God explicitly says he "did not command, nor did it enter my mind" (v.31). The rhetorical force of "it did not enter my mind" is devastating — God cannot even conceive of commanding such an act. The oracle pronounces that this valley of death will be renamed "the Valley of Slaughter" and will become an open cemetery when the dead overflow all burial capacity.

cross.note: Josiah defiled Topheth specifically to stop child sacrifice — but the practice evidently continued or resumed after his death.
Jesus' use of "Gehenna" (= gê hinnōm) draws directly on this prophetic tradition — the valley of child sacrifice becomes the image of eschatological judgment.
The Mosaic prohibition against giving children to Molech — the law that Judah was violating at Topheth.

notes.note: Child sacrifice to Molech is the ultimate covenant abomination. "They have set up their vile images in the house that bears my Name" — idols placed inside the temple itself. God's emphatic denial — "I did not command this, nor did it come into my mind" — distinguishes true worship from the monstrous distortions of pagan religion.

The Valley of Ben Hinnom will be renamed the Valley of Slaughter. The dead will be so numerous that burial space will be exhausted. Carcasses will become food for birds and beasts — the ultimate curse in ancient Near Eastern thought, where proper burial was essential for honor in death.

Calvin emphasizes God's words "nor did it enter my mind" as a refutation of any theology that attributes evil to divine will. God is utterly opposed to child sacrifice — it is a human invention, not a divine command, and those who practice it act against God, not for him.

The phrase "nor did it enter my mind" (wĕlōʾ ʿālĕtâ ʿal-libbî) is an emphatic denial of divine involvement. The expression is unique to Jeremiah (also 19:5; 32:35) and serves as God's definitive repudiation of child sacrifice.

The renaming from "Topheth/Valley of Ben Hinnom" to "Valley of Slaughter" transforms a place of cultic activity into a site of judgment. Name changes in prophetic literature signify fundamental transformation of identity and destiny.

Lundbom reads the Topheth oracle as the climax of the entire Temple Sermon. The structure moves from false temple theology (vv.1–15) through false worship (vv.16–29) to the most extreme form of religious perversion (vv.30–34). Child sacrifice is the reductio ad absurdum of a religion that has lost all connection to the God it claims to serve.

Brueggemann observes that the Topheth oracle collapses the distinction between worship and violence. When religion requires the death of children, the entire system has become demonic. The prophetic task is not reform but demolition — this structure cannot be repaired, only destroyed.

themes.note: Judgment peaks at 10 with the Valley of Slaughter oracle. Worship at 9 reflects the chapter's sustained critique of false worship — temple as talisman, queen of heaven cult, and child sacrifice. Holiness and Justice both at 8 underscore the ethical demands God makes in contrast to empty ritual.

rows.label: vv.16–20
rows.text: Do Not Pray: The Queen of Heaven

lit.note: The Temple Sermon is the structural hinge of Jeremiah 1–25: it transitions from the general covenant lawsuit (chs. 2–6) to specific institutional critique. The progression — false security → false worship → child sacrifice — traces the logic of apostasy to its most extreme conclusion.

hebtext.note: The people chant "the temple of the LORD, the temple of the LORD, the temple of the LORD" (hêkal YHWH × 3, v.4) — a liturgical formula treated as a talisman. ppl.name: Jeremiah
ppl.text: Jeremiah stands at the gate of the temple and attacks the foundational belief of Jerusalem theology: that God's presence in the temple guarantees the city's inviolability…

ppl.name: Moses
ppl.text: Moses stands at the centre of the Pentateuch’s narrative — the one man who spoke with God face to face (Exod 33:11; Num 12:8) and mediated the Sinai covenant to Israel. In Leviticus he receives the priestly legislation; in Numbers he leads, intercedes, and ultimately fails at Meribah (Num 20). The writer of Hebrews calls him "faithful in all God’s house" (Heb 3:5) while distinguishing him from Christ, the Son over the house.

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Temple Sermon

translations.text: If you really change your ways and your actions and deal with each other justly,
“For if you truly amend your ways and your deeds, if you truly execute justice one with another,
But look, you are trusting in deceptive words that are worthless.
“Behold, you trust in deceptive words to no avail.
